53	
54	# Blueprint Writer
55	
56	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
57	
58	## Your Job
59	
60	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
61	
62	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
63	
64	## Directive Format
65	
66	```markdown
67	# Blueprint Writer Directive
68	
69	## Slug
70	<slug>
71	
72	## Target chapter
73	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
74	
75	## Strategy context
76	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
77	
78	## Required content
79	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
80	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
81	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
82	- ...
83	
84	## Out of scope
85	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
86	
87	## References
88	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
89	- ...
90	
91	## Expected outcome
92	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
93	```
94	
95	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
96	
97	## Chapter format
98	
99	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
100	
101	```latex
102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
107	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
108	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
109	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
110	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
111	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
112	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
113	\end{theorem}
114	
115	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
116	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
117	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
118	\begin{proof}
119	  \uses{thm:another_result}
120	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
121	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
122	\end{proof}
123	```
124	
125	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
126	
127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
139	
140	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
143	
144	**The hard rule, explicit:**
145	
146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
147	
148	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
149	
150	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
151	
152	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
153	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
154	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
155	
156	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.

158	## Rules
159	
160	### What you CAN do
161	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
162	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
163	- Add `\uses{...}` cross-references.
164	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.

168	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
169	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.

If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
171	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
172	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). 183	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.

3	\chapter{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero (RR.2)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, IV \S1, pp.~294--297
7	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages
8	% 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below
9	% were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of
10	% the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
11	
12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter is the second of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
15	that close the project's headline RR bridge
16	\[
17	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
18	  \,\colon\,
19	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
20	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
21	\]
22	The bridge consumed by
23	REF of
24	\texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} is the Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain:
25	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) and the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
26	\to \mathbb Z$ (\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the
27	Riemann--Roch dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
28	\[
29	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
30	\]
31	(this chapter, \texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections identification
32	\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) (\texttt{RR.3}); the
33	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
34	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
35	
36	% NOTE (Serre duality vs. direct \(\chi\) — iter-173 sub-phase choice).
37	% This chapter proves the RR formula via the \emph{direct \(\chi\)
38	% inductive proof} (Hartshorne IV.1.3 step ``Next, let \(D\) be any
39	% divisor\ldots'', p.~296), \emph{not} via Serre duality. The reason is
40	% strictly Mathlib-side: Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships no
41	% dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and no Serre duality theorem
42	% \(H^i(X, \mathcal F) \cong H^{n-i}(X, \omega_X \otimes \mathcal F^\vee)^*\);
43	% routing through that machinery would force a multi-iter sub-build
44	% strictly heavier than the genus-\(0\) critical path requires. The
45	% direct \(\chi\) proof depends only on additivity of the Euler
46	% characteristic on a short exact sequence of coherent sheaves (a
47	% standard preadditive-category fact), the genus base case
48	% \(\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g\), and the closed-point skyscraper
49	% identity \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\).
50	
51	% NOTE (Genus-\(0\) carve-out vs. general \(g\) — iter-173 sub-phase
52	% choice). The chapter restricts the headline theorem
53	% REF to the genus-\(0\) specialisation
54	% \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\). The general-\(g\)
55	% statement \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) is downstream of
56	% \texttt{RR.2} (it requires the canonical divisor \(K\) and Serre
57	% duality / the \(K - D\) pairing) and is \emph{not} needed for the
58	% genus-\(0\) critical path: \texttt{RR.3} consumes only
59	% REF, and \texttt{RR.4} consumes
60	% \texttt{RR.3}. The intermediate \(\chi\)-identity
61	% REF is stated and
62	% sketched for general \(g\) (the inductive argument is genus-uniform);
63	% its full closure on a general-\(g\) curve waits for the project's
64	% subsequent Riemann--Roch chapter, but the proof body in
65	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} pins the
66	% \emph{statement} for general \(g\) and may close the body initially
67	% under a \(g = 0\) hypothesis.
68	
69	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
70	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
71	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
72	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean}) carry the same
73	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
74	\begin{quote}
75	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
76	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
77	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
78	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
79	\end{quote}
80	The genus \(g(C)\) is the one defined in REF of
81	\texttt{Genus.tex}, namely \(g(C) = \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\)
82	via the project's \(\Module\bar k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology
83	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}).
84	
85	\section{The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve}
86	
87	\begin{definition}
88	
89	
90	\leanok
91	[Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve]
92	  \label{def:eulerChar_curve}
93	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic}
94	  
95	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(\chi(\mathcal F)\)
96	  % inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
97	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
98	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
99	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
100	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
101	  % is the Euler characteristic
102	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
103	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295, the formula displayed
104	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3.}
105	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F\)
106	  be a coherent sheaf on \(C\). The \emph{Euler characteristic} of
107	  \(\mathcal F\) is the integer
108	  \[
109	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;:=\;
110	    \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \,\dim_{\bar k} H^i(C, \mathcal F)
111	    \;=\;
112	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal F)
113	    \,-\,
114	    \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal F).
115	  \]
116	  The second equality is a curve-specific simplification: because
117	  \(\dim C = 1\), Grothendieck vanishing (Hartshorne~III.2.7) gives
118	  \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\), so the alternating sum has
119	  only two terms. Coherence of \(\mathcal F\) on the proper $\bar
120	  k\(-scheme \)C\( guarantees that both \)H^0\( and \)H^1$ are
121	  finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology
122	  finiteness theorem, the same finiteness backing
123	  REF), so the two terms are honest natural numbers and
124	  the difference is a well-defined integer.
125	
126	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition

183	\section{The \(\chi\) identity: \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\)}
184	
185	\begin{theorem}
186	
187	
188	\leanok
189	[Euler-characteristic identity for \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)]
190	  \label{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus}
191	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus}
192	  
193	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Theorem~1.3 (the reduction inside the
194	  % proof: ``Thus we have to show that for any \(D\), $\chi(\mathscr L(D))
195	  % = \deg D + 1 - g$'') (read from
196	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
197	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
198	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
199	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
200	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
201	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
202	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
203	  % is the Euler characteristic
204	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
205	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295.}
206	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
207	  \(\bar k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). Then
208	  \[
209	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g.
210	  \]
211	\end{theorem}
212	
213	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "First we consider the case \(D = 0\). Then our
214	% formula says
215	% \(\dim H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = 1 - g\),
216	% because \(H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) = k\) for any projective variety
217	% (I, 3.4), and \(\dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = g\) by (1.1).
218	% Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point. We will show
219	% that the formula is true for \(D\) if and only if it is true for
220	% \(D + P\). Since any divisor can be reached from \(0\) in a finite number
221	% of steps by adding or subtracting a point each time, this will show
222	% the result holds for all \(D\).
223	% We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its structure sheaf is
224	% the skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\), which we denote by
225	% \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\) by (II, 6.18).
226	% Therefore we have an exact sequence
227	% \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
228	% Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
229	% \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
230	% (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
231	% does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\)). Now the Euler characteristic is
232	% additive on short exact sequences (III, Ex. 5.1), and $\chi(k(P))
233	% = 1$, so we have
234	% \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\).
235	% On the other hand, \(\deg D + P = \deg D + 1\), so our formula is true
236	% for \(D\) if and only if it is true for \(D + P\), as required."
237	\begin{proof}
238	  \leanok
239	  
240	  The proof is by induction on the structure of \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) as the free
241	  abelian group on closed points (REF,
242	  REF). Both sides of the asserted
243	  equation are additive in \(D\) (the right-hand side by linearity of
244	  \(\deg\), REF; the left-hand side by the
245	  inductive step proved below), so it suffices to verify the formula
246	  for \(D = 0\) and to show that it transports across the elementary
247	  modification \(D \mapsto D + [P]\) at any closed point \(P \in C\).
248	
249	  \emph{Base case \(D = 0\).} The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(0)\) is the
250	  structure sheaf \(\mathcal O_C\), and the formula
251	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g\) reads
252	  \[
253	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
254	    - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)
255	    \;=\; 1 - g.
256	  \]
257	  The second term is the definition of the genus,
258	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g\) (REF). The
259	  first term is \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\): \(C\) is a
260	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over the algebraically
261	  closed field \(\bar k\), so its connected components agree with its
262	  geometrically connected components, and $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
263	  \cong \bar k\( as \)\bar k$-vector spaces (the Hartshorne~I.3.4
264	  statement that ``\(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X) = k\) for any projective
265	  variety'' over an algebraically closed field; the Mathlib home is
266	  the project's \(H^0\)-bridge in
267	  \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}). Substituting these
268	  two values gives \(1 - g = 1 - g\), as required.
269	
270	  \emph{Inductive step (Hartshorne's ``\(D \leftrightsquigarrow D + P\)''
271	  step).} Let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. We follow Hartshorne's
272	  argument: view \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(C\); its structure
273	  sheaf is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\) supported at \(P\),
274	  and its sheaf of ideals is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C\)
275	  (the locally principal ideal sheaf cutting out \(P\) in \(C\)). Hence
276	  the short exact sequence
277	  \[
278	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
279	  \]
280	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\) (Hartshorne~II.6.18). Tensoring with the
281	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) (a locally free
282	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module of rank \(1\), so tensoring preserves exactness
283	  and leaves the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged) yields
284	  \[
285	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(D)
286	    \to \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
287	    \to k(P) \to 0.
288	  \]
289	  Additivity of the Euler characteristic on a short exact sequence
290	  of coherent sheaves (Mathlib's preadditive-category Euler
291	  characteristic; see the Lean reference note at the end of this
292	  proof) gives
293	  \[
294	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P]))
295	    \;=\;
296	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + \chi(k(P)).
297	  \]
298	  The skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) has
299	  \(H^0(C, k(P)) \cong \bar k\) and \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\) (the cohomology
300	  of a flasque sheaf supported at a closed point is concentrated in
301	  degree \(0\), with \(H^0\) equal to the stalk \(\bar k\)), so
302	  \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\). Therefore
303	  \[
304	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + 1.
305	  \]
306	  On the other hand, \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
307	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), so by
308	  REF
309	  \[
310	    \deg(D + [P]) + 1 - g \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g + 1.
311	  \]
312	  Comparing the two displayed identities, the formula
313	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) holds for \(D\) if and only
314	  if it holds for \(D + [P]\). Since the closed-point divisors \([P]\)
315	  generate \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) as a free abelian group, the formula propagates
316	  from \(D = 0\) to every \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) by induction on the (signed)
317	  number of generators in the expression of \(D\).
318	
319	  \emph{Lean reference note on \(\chi\)-additivity.} Mathlib (snapshot
320	  \texttt{b80f227}) provides the Euler-characteristic API
321	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.EulerCharacteristic}
322	  (\texttt{HomologicalComplex.eulerChar}, \texttt{GradedObject.eulerChar})
323	  but does \emph{not} ship the specific additivity statement
324	  \texttt{CategoryTheory.ShortExact.eulerChar\_additive} [expected,
325	  not in Mathlib under that name]. The genus-\(0\) proof of
326	  REF therefore threads additivity of
327	  \(\chi\) on a short exact sequence either as a project-side lemma
328	  proved inside \texttt{RRFormula.lean} (from
329	  \texttt{Submodule.finrank\_quotient\_add\_finrank} combined with
330	  the long exact sequence of \(H^*\)) or as a candidate Mathlib upstream
331	  PR. The base case \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\) is closed by the Mathlib
332	  skyscraper-sheaf API together with the project's \(H^0\)-bridge
333	  (REF).
334	\end{proof}

336	\section{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero}
337	
338	\begin{theorem}
339	
340	
341	\leanok
342	[Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero]
343	  \label{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}
344	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero}
345	  
346	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Example~1.3.5 (genus-\(0\) specialisation
347	  % of Riemann--Roch; the curve-is-rational consequence) (read from
348	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
349	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is \emph{rational}
350	  % if it is birational to \(\mathbf P^1\) (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in
351	  % this chapter are complete and nonsingular by definition, a curve
352	  % \(X\) is rational if and only if \(X \cong \mathbf P^1\) (I, 6.12). Now
353	  % using (1.3) we can show that \(X\) is rational if and only if \(g = 0\).
354	  % We already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) (I, Ex. 7.2), so
355	  % conversely suppose given a curve \(X\) of genus 0. Let \(P, Q\) be two
356	  % distinct points of \(X\) and apply Riemann--Roch to the divisor
357	  % \(D = P - Q\). Since \(\deg K - D = -2\), using (1.3.3) above, we have
358	  % \(l(K - D) = 0\), and so we find \(l(D) = 1\). But \(D\) is a divisor of
359	  % degree 0, so by (1.2) we have \(D \sim 0\), in other words $P \sim
360	  % Q\(. But this implies \)X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
361	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
362	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
363	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) with
364	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\). Then
365	  \[
366	    \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
367	  \]
368	\end{theorem}
369	
370	\begin{proof}
371	  \leanok
372	  
373	  Specialising
374	  REF to the genus-\(0\)
375	  hypothesis \(g = 0\) yields
376	  \[
377	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
378	  \]
379	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF and the definition
380	  \(\ell(D) = \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))\)
381	  (REF), this rewrites as
382	  \[
383	    \ell(D) - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
384	    \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
385	  \]
386	  Under the standing genus-\(0\) hypothesis and the assumption
387	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\), the higher-cohomology term vanishes:
388	  \[
389	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; 0
390	    \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0.
391	  \]
392	  Substituting yields \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\), as required.
393	
394	  \emph{The \(H^1\)-vanishing input.} On a smooth proper geometrically
395	  irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), the vanishing
396	  \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) is the Hartshorne
397	  formulation Lemma~1.2 read covariantly: \(\ell(D) \neq 0\) forces
398	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\); equivalently, for a curve where every
399	  degree-\(0\)-or-higher invertible sheaf is generated by global
400	  sections after a single point-twist, \(H^1\) of the twist must vanish
401	  by the long exact sequence of the skyscraper extension at any
402	  closed point. In the chain of sub-build chapters, this \(H^1\)
403	  vanishing is the natural content of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
404	  \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added), where the line
405	  bundle \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) and its cohomology are constructed; once
406	  \texttt{RR.3} is closed, the present theorem inherits the
407	  vanishing as an instance, and the proof closes verbatim as
408	  displayed. Pending \texttt{RR.3}, the Lean signature
409	  \texttt{l\_eq\_degree\_plus\_one\_of\_genus\_zero} threads the
410	  \(H^1\)-vanishing hypothesis explicitly as a named premise; the
411	  iteration that closes \texttt{RR.3} then drops the premise in a
412	  follow-up signature simplification.
413	
414	  \emph{Sub-build note for \texttt{RR.4}.} The downstream consumer of
415	  this theorem is the genus-\(0\) classification
416	  REF of
417	  \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}: with \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\)
418	  and the degree-zero principal-divisor identity
419	  (REF), Hartshorne's Example~1.3.5 chain
420	  produces a degree-\(1\) morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) via
421	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} applied to the two-dimensional
422	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\), which is then upgraded to an
423	  isomorphism in \texttt{RR.4}.
424	\end{proof}

101	/-! ## §1. The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve -/
102	
103	/-- **Euler characteristic of a `ModuleCat k`-valued sheaf on a smooth proper
104	curve `C / k̄`.**
105	
106	On a curve (one-dimensional scheme), Grothendieck vanishing
107	(Hartshorne III.2.7) gives `H^i(C, 𝓕) = 0` for `i ≥ 2`, so the classical
108	alternating sum
109	`χ(𝓕) = Σ_{i ≥ 0} (-1)^i dim_{k̄} H^i(C, 𝓕)` collapses to the two-term
110	expression
111	`χ(𝓕) = dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝓕) − dim_{k̄} H¹(C, 𝓕)`.
112	
113	This is the definition we ship. Coherence of `𝓕` on the proper `k̄`-scheme
114	`C` guarantees that both `H⁰` and `H¹` are finite-dimensional `k̄`-vector
115	spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology finiteness theorem, the same
116	finiteness backing `AlgebraicGeometry.genus`), so the two `Module.finrank`s
117	are honest natural numbers and the difference is a well-defined integer.
118	
119	The `Module k̄`-valued cohomology pipeline is the project's
120	`Scheme.HModule` (iter-009), the same wrapper used by
121	`AlgebraicGeometry.genus`.
122	
123	Blueprint reference: `def:eulerChar_curve`
124	(Hartshorne IV.1 p. 295, displayed inside the proof of Theorem 1.3). -/
125	noncomputable def Scheme.eulerCharacteristic
126	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
127	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
128	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
129	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
130	    (F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
131	      (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) : ℤ :=
132	  (Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar F 0) : ℤ)
133	    - (Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar F 1) : ℤ)
134

136

138	(`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`, `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean`),
139	where the locally-principal ideal sheaves of closed points are glued
140	into an invertible `𝒪_C`-module. **iter-183 Lane K** opened that file
141	with the typed-`sorry` pin `Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf` plus the three
142	immediate corollaries `sheafOf_zero`, `sheafOf_singlePoint`, and
143	`sheafOf_ses_single_add`. **iter-183 Lane H** (this file) retires the
144	former local `sheafOf` typed-`sorry` placeholder (previously at L168 in

146	`ℓ`-invariant, the χ-identity, the genus-`0` Riemann–Roch formula) now

151	variable {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
152	  {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsProper C.hom]

156	/-! ## §3. The `ℓ`-invariant of a Weil divisor -/
157	
158	/-- **The `ℓ`-invariant of a Weil divisor `D` on a smooth proper curve
159	`C / k̄`.**
160	
161	By definition,
162	`ℓ(D) := dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(D)) ∈ ℕ`,
163	the `k̄`-dimension of the space of global sections of the invertible sheaf
164	`𝒪_C(D)` associated to `D`. Finiteness of `ℓ(D)` is a consequence of the
165	coherent-cohomology finiteness theorem on a proper `k̄`-scheme
166	(Hartshorne II.5.19 / III.5.2), the same input that backs the finiteness
167	of `genus C`.
168	
169	The classical *complete linear system* `|D|` has `k̄`-projective dimension
170	`ℓ(D) − 1`; the chapter never uses this projective interpretation —
171	`ℓ(D)` is the only quantity consumed by the Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain.
172	
173	Blueprint reference: `def:l_invariant` (Hartshorne IV.1 p. 295,
174	"We denote `dim_k H⁰(X, 𝒪(D))` by `l(D)`"). -/
175	noncomputable def l (D : C.left.WeilDivisor) : ℕ :=
176	  Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar (sheafOf (C := C) D) 0)
177	
178	end Scheme.WeilDivisor

180	/-! ## §4. The χ-identity: `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g`
181	
182	The bridge from the structural Euler characteristic to the arithmetic
183	degree of a divisor. Hartshorne IV.1 Theorem 1.3 reduces Riemann–Roch to
184	this identity; the proof is the inductive `D ↔ D + [P]` step (additivity
185	of χ on the closed-point short exact sequence, with base case `D = 0`
186	giving `χ(𝒪_C) = 1 − g` from `dim H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1` and the definition of
187	the genus). The closure of the body is iter-175+ work after the
188	`Euler-characteristic additivity on a short exact sequence`-style
189	project-side helper is supplied and the `RR.3` sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` has a
190	body.
191	
192	**Iter-181 Lane H factoring.** Both Hartshorne IV.1.3 inputs (the base
193	case `χ(𝒪_C) = 1 − g` and the inductive step `χ(𝒪_C(D + Y)) = χ(𝒪_C(D))
194	+ n`) intrinsically reference the body of `Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf`,
195	which is still a typed-`sorry` placeholder waiting on the sibling
196	chapter `RR.3` (`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`). We therefore factor the proof
197	into two named substantive helper lemmas (3-tier disclosure: **honest
198	named-sorry helpers** — each helper has a substantive type encoding a
199	nontrivial mathematical claim about the `sheafOf` line bundle whose
200	closure is downstream of the `RR.3` body):
201	
202	1. `eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_zero` — base case `χ(sheafOf 0) = 1 − g`,
203	   which on closure of `RR.3` reduces to `sheafOf 0 = toModuleKSheaf C`
204	   plus the Hartshorne I.3.4 input `dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1` plus the
205	   definition of the genus.
206	2. `eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_single_add` — inductive step
207	   `χ(sheafOf (Finsupp.single Y n + D)) = χ(sheafOf D) + n` for any
208	   `Y : C.left.PrimeDivisor` and `n : ℤ`. On closure of `RR.3` this
209	   reduces to the Hartshorne IV.1.3 SES additivity argument iterated

213	`D : C.left.WeilDivisor` (via `Finsupp.induction`), using the two
214	helpers and the additivity of `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree`. -/

217	On a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄`, the global
218	sections of the structure sheaf form a one-dimensional `k̄`-vector space:

233	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
234	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]

236	    Module.finrank kbar
237	        (Scheme.HModule kbar (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C) 0) = 1 := by
238	  -- Step 1: H⁰-bridge LinearEquiv chain (existing project infrastructure).
239	  -- `HModule kbar (toModuleKSheaf C) 0` is identified, step by step, with
240	  -- the underlying global-sections ring `C.left.presheaf.obj (op ⊤)` via:
241	  --   `HModule_zero_linearEquiv`         -- Ext₀ ≃ Hom from constant sheaf;
242	  --   `constantSheafGammaHom_linearEquiv` -- constant-sheaf-Γ adjunction;
243	  --   `homFromOne_linearEquiv`            -- Hom-from-`k` evaluation at `1`;
244	  --   `SheafGammaObj_linearEquiv_top`     -- `Sheaf.Γ` reads the top section.
245	  let LE1 :=
246	    Scheme.HModule_zero_linearEquiv kbar (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C)
247	  let LE2 :=
248	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.constantSheafGammaHom_linearEquiv kbar

250	      (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C)

252	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.homFromOne_linearEquiv kbar
253	      ((Sheaf.Γ (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
254	        (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C))
255	  let LE4 :=
256	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.SheafGammaObj_linearEquiv_top kbar
257	      (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C)
258	  let LE := ((LE1.trans LE2).trans LE3).trans LE4
259	  rw [LE.finrank_eq]
260	  -- Step 2: `(toModuleKSheaf C).obj.obj (op ⊤)` is definitionally
261	  -- `ModuleCat.of kbar (C.left.presheaf.obj (op ⊤))` (per `toModuleKPresheaf_obj`),
262	  -- so we transport to the global-sections ring and apply
263	  -- `Module.finrank_of_bijective_algebraMap` + `IsAlgClosed.algebraMap_bijective_of_isIntegral`.
264	  -- Convert the ModuleCat coercion to the underlying global sections ring.
265	  change Module.finrank kbar
266	      (C.left.presheaf.obj
267	        (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat))) = 1
268	  -- Module-finiteness via the iter-044 Stein input.
269	  haveI hFin : Module.Finite kbar
270	      (C.left.presheaf.obj
271	        (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat))) :=
272	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.module_finite_globalSections_of_isProper kbar C

279	  -- it inline via the curve nonemptiness, sidestepping the `↥↑` / `↑↑` coercion
280	  -- spelling mismatch that defeats `haveI`-based instance registration.
281	  haveI hDom : IsDomain
282	      (C.left.presheaf.obj
283	        (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat))) :=
284	    @AlgebraicGeometry.IsIntegral.component_integral
285	      C.left _ (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)
286	      ⟨⟨hNECurve.some, Set.mem_univ _⟩⟩
287	  -- `Module.Finite kbar A` → `Algebra.IsIntegral kbar A` (auto-instance).

289	  exact Module.finrank_of_bijective_algebraMap
290	    (IsAlgClosed.algebraMap_bijective_of_isIntegral (k := kbar)
291	      (K := C.left.presheaf.obj
292	        (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat))))

299	`χ(S.X₂) = χ(S.X₁) + χ(S.X₃)`.

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
33	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
34	constructions are functorial in \(C\) and do not formally use these hypotheses
35	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
36	chapters). A test object is a \(k\)-scheme \(T\); the relative curve over \(T\) is
37	\(C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T\), with \(\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C\) the
38	companion projection. Both \(\pi_T\) and \(\pi_C\) are flat (because base change of
39	flat morphisms is flat and \(C/k\) is flat since \(k\) is a field).
40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the total space, and the
43	Picard group \(\Pic(T)\) of the base. The pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\) sends a line
44	bundle on \(T\) to its base-change line bundle on \(C \times_k T\); the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\) that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
54

112	\section{The pullback functor along the projection}
113	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_projection}
114	
115	If \(f : Y \to Z\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
116	\(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module, the pullback \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
117	\(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-module (the pullback of an open cover trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\)
118	trivialises \(f^*\mathcal{M}\), and the pullback of a free module of rank one is
119	free of rank one). This is a standard preservation fact (Stacks tag 01HH); we
120	specialise it to the projection \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and the
121	\(\mathcal{O}_T\)-Picard group.
122	
123	\begin{definition}\leanok
124	[Pullback along the projection]
125	  \label{def:pullback_along_projection}
126	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
127	  
128	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
129	  % Definition df:aPf (functoriality of the absolute Picard functor)
130	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1290).
131	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
132	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
133	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
134	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
135	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
136	  %  [\dots]
137	  %  The absolute Picard functor  is a ``prepared presheaf'' in this
138	  % sense: given any family of  \(S\)-schemes \(T_i\), we have
139	  %     \(\)\Pic_X\bigl(\textstyle\coprod T_i\bigr)=\prod\Pic_X(T_i).\(\)"
140	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
141	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' and the ``prepared presheaf'' paragraph
142	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HH).}
143	  Let \(C/k\) and \(T/k\) be as in
144	  \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\)
145	  denote the canonical projection. The \emph{pullback along \(\pi_T\)} is the
146	  group homomorphism (in fact, the set map; the additive structure is not used
147	  in this chapter)
148	  \[
149	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T),
150	    \qquad [\mathcal{N}] \;\longmapsto\; [\pi_T^* \mathcal{N}],
151	  \]
152	  sending an isomorphism class of an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module
153	  \(\mathcal{N}\) to the isomorphism class of \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N}\), an invertible
154	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module (Stacks tag 01HH). This assignment is well
155	  defined because pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules respects isomorphism, and is
156	  multiplicative on tensor products. It defines the functor
157	  \(\pi^* \mathrm{Pic} \subseteq \Pic_C\) that we quotient by in the definition of
158	  the relative Picard presheaf.

\section{The \(\chi\) identity: \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\)}

\subsection*{Substrate lemmas for the proof of the \(\chi\)-identity}

The proof of REF below
decomposes into three substantive substrate lemmas, factored out of the
Hartshorne~IV.1.3 inductive argument. The first
(REF, \emph{additivity}) is the
classical statement that \(\chi\) descends to the Grothendieck group
of \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\); the second
(REF, \emph{iso-invariance}) is the
elementary fact that \(\chi\) is well defined on isomorphism classes
of sheaves; the third
(REF, \emph{skyscraper}) computes \(\chi\)
on a closed-point skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\). Pinning each as its own
Lean declaration lets the prover attack the three substrate gaps
independently of the parent inductive argument.

\begin{lemma}[Additivity of the Euler characteristic on a short exact sequence]
  \label{lem:euler_char_shortExact_add}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add}
  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (inside the proof of Theorem~1.3,
  % the citation ``III, Ex.~5.1'' for the additivity of \(\chi\) on a
  % short exact sequence) (read from
  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
  % exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1), and \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), so we have
  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\)."
  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1 p.~296 (citation of III, Ex.~5.1
  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3).}
  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
  \(\bar k\), and let
  \[
    0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0
  \]
  be a short exact sequence in the category of \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
  sheaves on \(C\). Then
  \[
    \chi(\mathcal F_2) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3).
  \]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
  Apply the project-side \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured covariant Ext-LES
  (Mathlib's \texttt{Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence}) to the constant-sheaf
  test object \(L = (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
  \bar k\;\bar k)\) and the short exact sequence
  \(0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0\). The
  resulting nine-term sequence
  \[
    H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to H^0(\mathcal F_3)
    \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2) \to H^1(\mathcal F_3)
    \to H^2(\mathcal F_1) \to H^2(\mathcal F_2) \to H^2(\mathcal F_3)
  \]
  is exact in the category of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces. Grothendieck
  vanishing \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\) on the
  one-dimensional scheme \(C\) (Hartshorne~III.2.7) truncates the
  sequence to a six-term exact sequence
  \[
    0 \to K_0 \to H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to
    H^0(\mathcal F_3) \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2)
    \to H^1(\mathcal F_3) \to 0
  \]
  (with \(K_0 = 0\) the kernel of the first map, kept here only as a
  bookkeeping placeholder). Rank-counting on a six-term exact sequence
  of finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces — iterated applications
  of \(\mathtt{Submodule.finrank\_quotient\_add\_finrank}\) at each
  intermediate kernel/image quotient — yields the alternating identity
  \[
    \dim H^0(\mathcal F_1) - \dim H^0(\mathcal F_2) + \dim H^0(\mathcal F_3)

\begin{lemma}[Iso-invariance of the Euler characteristic]
  \label{lem:euler_char_iso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_iso}
  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
  \textit{Project-bespoke decomposition} (Archon-original packaging of
  the Mathlib \(\Ext\)-postcomposition API; no external source named).
  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
  \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F, \mathcal G\) be \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
  sheaves on \(C\) with an isomorphism \(\mathcal F \cong \mathcal G\).
  Then
  \[
    \chi(\mathcal F) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal G).
  \]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
  An isomorphism \(e \colon \mathcal F \xrightarrow{\;\cong\;} \mathcal G\)
  induces, in each cohomological degree \(n \in \mathbb N\), a
  \(\bar k\)-linear equivalence
  \[
    H^n(C, \mathcal F) \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\; H^n(C, \mathcal G)
  \]
  by post-composition with the Ext-degree-zero classes
  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{hom})\) and
  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{inv})\). The mutual-inverse identities
  for this pair of post-compositions reduce — via the associativity
  lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.comp\_assoc\_of\_third\_deg\_zero}\), the
  composition lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0\mathtt{\_comp\_mk}_0\), and the
  iso identities \(e.\mathtt{hom} \circ e.\mathtt{inv} = \mathrm{id}\),
  \(e.\mathtt{inv} \circ e.\mathtt{hom} = \mathrm{id}\) — to the identity
  map on \(\Ext\) elements. Specialising to \(n = 0\) and \(n = 1\) and
  applying \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.finrank\_eq}\) at each of those two
  degrees gives
  \(\dim H^n(C, \mathcal F) = \dim H^n(C, \mathcal G)\) for
  \(n \in \{0, 1\}\). Unfolding the definition
  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
  (REF) closes the goal.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Euler characteristic of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf]
  \label{lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_skyscraperSheaf}
  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (the \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\) input
  % cited inline inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
  % exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1), and \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), so we have
  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\)."
  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1 p.~296 (the ``\(\chi(k(P)) = 1\)''
  input inside the proof of Theorem~1.3).}
  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Write \(k(P)\) for
  the closed-point skyscraper sheaf
  \[
    k(P) \;:=\; \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
    (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k),
  \]
  the \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf on \(C\) with stalk \(\bar k\) at
  \(P\) and zero stalks elsewhere. Then
  \[
    \chi(k(P)) \;=\; 1.
  \]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
  By the definition of \(\chi\)
  (REF),
  \[
    \chi(k(P)) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P))
    \;-\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)).
  \]
  The closure decomposes cleanly into two independent half-statements,
  attacked separately by the prover.

  \emph{H\(^0\) half} — \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\).
  Compose the project's three-step \(\bar k\)-linear-equivalence chain
  \[
    H^0(C, k(P))
    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\; \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Sh}}\bigl((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k),\, k(P)\bigr)
    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\; (\mathtt{Sheaf}.\Gamma)(k(P))
    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\; (k(P))_\mathtt{val}(\mathop{\mathrm{op}}\top),
  \]
  via the project lemmas
  \texttt{Scheme.HModule\_zero\_linearEquiv},
  \texttt{Scheme.constantSheafGammaHom\_linearEquiv}, and
  \texttt{Scheme.SheafGammaObj\_linearEquiv\_top}. Applying
  \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.finrank\_eq}\) along this chain reduces the
  goal to
  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\;
  ((k(P))_\mathtt{val}(\mathop{\mathrm{op}}\top)) = 1\). The closed
  point \(P\) lies in the top open \(\top\) (the underlying space of
  \(C\)), so the skyscraper presheaf evaluates at \(\top\) to
  \(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k\) by definition, and
  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\;\bar k = 1\) closes the half.
  This half is axiom-clean closure projection
  (\(\sim 30\text{--}60\) LOC) against the existing project chain; it
  is the natural target for the iteration that follows the present
  blueprint expansion.

  \emph{H\(^1\) half} — \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
  Mathematically, the closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque (any
  pushforward of a sheaf from a one-point space is flasque;
  Hartshorne~III.2.5(c)), and flasque sheaves have vanishing higher
  cohomology in degrees \(\geq 1\) on any topological space
  (Hartshorne~III.2.5). Hence \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\) as a
  \(\bar k\)-vector space.

  % NOTE: gated on Mathlib flasque cohomology.
  % Mathlib `b80f227` does NOT ship an `H^n(X, F) = 0 for flasque F`
  % lemma at the `ModuleCat`-flavoured `Abelian.Ext`-cohomology level
  % used by the project's `Scheme.HModule kbar` pipeline. The closure
  % depends on either (a) an upstream Mathlib
  % `Sheaf.flasque_vanishing_cohomology` contribution at the
  % `Ext`-cohomology level for sheaves with values in an abelian
  % category with enough injectives, or (b) a project-side flasque-
  % cohomology bridge (~150-300 LOC, off the genus-0 critical path).
  % Until one of the two lands, the H¹ half is a typed-sorry blocker
  % independent of the H⁰ half — i.e. the prover can attack the H⁰
  % half against the existing project chain without waiting for the
  % H¹ machinery, and the present skyscraper lemma waits for the H¹
  % gap to clear.

  Combining the two halves through the definition of \(\chi\),
  \(\chi(k(P)) = 1 - 0 = 1\), closes the lemma.
\end{proof}
